title: Konkani language

infobox.value: [Southern](/wiki/Southern_India "Southern India") and [Western India](/wiki/Western_India "Western India") ([Konkan region](/wiki/Konkan_region "Konkan region"))  * [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa") * coastal areas of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra") and some parts of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") * [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") (in the [Dang district](/wiki/Dang_district,_Gujarat "Dang district, Gujarat")) * [Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu](/wiki/Dadra_and_Nagar_Haveli_and_Daman_and_Diu "Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu")[[2]](#cite_note-kenya-2)[[3]](#cite_note-curzon-3)

[Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages")  * [Indo-Iranian](/wiki/Indo-Iranian_languages "Indo-Iranian languages")   + [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages")     - [Southern Zone](/wiki/Southern_Indo-Aryan_languages "Southern Indo-Aryan languages")       * [Marathi–Konkani](/wiki/Marathi%E2%80%93Konkani_languages "Marathi–Konkani languages")         + **Konkani**

* **Dialect groups**: Karnataka Konkani, Goan Konkani, [Maharashtrian Konkani](/wiki/Maharashtrian_Konkani "Maharashtrian Konkani"), Kerala Konkani * **Individual dialects**: Dangi, [Malvani](/wiki/Malvani_dialect "Malvani dialect"), Chitpavani, Antruz, Bardeskari, Saxtti, [Nawayati](/wiki/Nawayathi_dialect "Nawayathi dialect"), Mangalorean Catholic, [Southern Saraswat](/wiki/Southern_Saraswat_Konkani "Southern Saraswat Konkani"), Daldi (Nakhuda dialect), Pednekari, Koli and [Aagri](/wiki/Agri_dialect "Agri dialect")[[6]](#cite_note-6)

* Present   + [Devanagari script](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") ([Balbodh](/wiki/Balbodh "Balbodh"), official)   + [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script")[[note 1]](#cite_note-7)   + [Kannada script](/wiki/Kannada_script "Kannada script")[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1)[[note 2]](#cite_note-8)   + [Malayalam script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script")[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1)   + [Perso-Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script")[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1) * Past   + [Nāgarī](/wiki/N%C4%81gar%C4%AB_script "Nāgarī script")   + [Goykanadi](/wiki/Goykanadi "Goykanadi")   + [Modi script](/wiki/Modi_script "Modi script")

[Karnataka Konkani Sahitya Academy](/wiki/Karnataka_Konkani_Sahitya_Academy "Karnataka Konkani Sahitya Academy") and the [Government of Goa](/wiki/Government_of_Goa "Government of Goa")[[8]](#cite_note-official-10)
`kok` – inclusive code Individual codes: `gom` – Goan Konkani `knn` – [Maharashtrian Konkani](/wiki/Maharashtrian_Konkani "Maharashtrian Konkani")

Karnataka Konkani, Goan Konkani, [Maharashtrian Konkani](/wiki/Maharashtrian_Konkani "Maharashtrian Konkani"), Kerala Konkani * **Individual dialects**: Dangi, [Malvani](/wiki/Malvani_dialect "Malvani dialect"), Chitpavani, Antruz, Bardeskari, Saxtti, [Nawayati](/wiki/Nawayathi_dialect "Nawayathi dialect"), Mangalorean Catholic, [Southern Saraswat](/wiki/Southern_Saraswat_Konkani "Southern Saraswat Konkani"), Daldi (Nakhuda dialect), Pednekari, Koli and [Aagri](/wiki/Agri_dialect "Agri dialect")[[6]](#cite_note-6) |

or **Concanese**, is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") spoken by the [Konkani people](/wiki/Konkani_people "Konkani people"), primarily in the [Konkan](/wiki/Konkan "Konkan") region, along the western [coast of India](/wiki/Coast_of_India "Coast of India"). It is one of the 22 [scheduled languages](/wiki/Scheduled_languages_of_India "Scheduled languages of India") mentioned in the [Indian Constitution](/wiki/Indian_Constitution "Indian Constitution"),[[10]](#cite_note-14) and the official language of the Indian state of [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"). It is also spoken in [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"), [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"),[[11]](#cite_note-15) [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat")[[12]](#cite_note-16) as well as [Daman, Diu & Silvassa](/wiki/Dadra_and_Nagar_Haveli_and_Daman_and_Diu "Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu").[[13]](#cite_note-17)

**Konkani**[[note 3]](#cite_note-12)[[a]](#cite_note-13) ([/ˈkɒŋkəni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KONG-kə-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), formerly **Concani** or **Concanese**, is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") spoken by the [Konkani people](/wiki/Konkani_people "Konkani people"), primarily in the [Konkan](/wiki/Konkan "Konkan") region, along the western [coast of India](/wiki/Coast_of_India "Coast of India"). It is one of the 22 [scheduled languages](/wiki/Scheduled_languages_of_India "Scheduled languages of India") mentioned in the [Indian Constitution](/wiki/Indian_Constitution "Indian Constitution"),[[10]](#cite_note-14) and the official language of the Indian state of [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"). It is also spoken in [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"), [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"),[[11]](#cite_note-15) [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat")[[12]](#cite_note-16) as well as [Daman, Diu & Silvassa](/wiki/Dadra_and_Nagar_Haveli_and_Daman_and_Diu "Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu").[[13]](#cite_note-17)

Konkani is a member of the [Southern Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages#Southern_Zone "Indo-Aryan languages") group. It retains elements of [Vedic](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit") structures and shows similarities with both [Western](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages#Western_Zone "Indo-Aryan languages") and [Eastern Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages#Eastern_Zone "Indo-Aryan languages").[[14]](#cite_note-18) The first known Konkani inscription, dated to the 2nd century AD and sometimes claimed as "[Old Marathi](/wiki/Old_Marathi "Old Marathi")" is the one at [Arvalem](/wiki/List_of_Monuments_of_National_Importance_in_Goa#List_of_monuments_of_national_importance "List of Monuments of National Importance in Goa");[[15]](#cite_note-19)[[16]](#cite_note-20) the second oldest Konkani inscription, is one of those at [Shravanabelagola](/wiki/Shravanabelagola "Shravanabelagola"), dated to between 981 AD and 1117 AD, it was wrongly touted as "Old Marathi" from the time it was discovered and interpreted.[[17]](#cite_note-21)[[18]](#cite_note-22)[[19]](#cite_note-23) Other Konkani inscriptions are found scattered across the Konkan region, especially from [Kurla](/wiki/Kurla "Kurla")[[20]](#cite_note-24) in [Bombay (Mumbai)](/wiki/Bombay_(Mumbai) "Bombay (Mumbai)") to [Ponda, Goa](/wiki/Ponda,_Goa "Ponda, Goa").[[21]](#cite_note-25)[[22]](#cite_note-goatourism.gov.in-26)

Many Konkani dialects are spoken along and beyond the Konkan region, from [Damaon](/wiki/Daman,_India "Daman, India") in the north to [Karwar](/wiki/Karwar "Karwar") in the south; most of which are only partially mutually intelligible with one another due to a lack of linguistic contact and exchanges with the standard and principal forms of Konkani. It is also spoken by migrants outside of the Konkan proper, in [Nagpore](/wiki/Nagpore "Nagpore"), [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat"), [Cochin](/wiki/Cochin "Cochin"), [Mangalore](/wiki/Mangalore "Mangalore"), [Ahmedabad](/wiki/Ahmedabad "Ahmedabad"), [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"), [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), etc.[[23]](#cite_note-27)[[24]](#cite_note-28)[[25]](#cite_note-29)[[26]](#cite_note-30)[[27]](#cite_note-31) Dialects such as [Malvani](/wiki/Malvani_language "Malvani language"), Chitpavani, and Damani[[28]](#cite_note-32) in Maharashtra are threatened by [language assimilation](/wiki/Language_assimilation "Language assimilation") into the linguistic majority of non-Konkani [states and territories of India](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_India "States and territories of India").[[29]](#cite_note-33)[[30]](#cite_note-34)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Classification")]

Konkani belongs to the [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") branch. It is part of the [Marathi-Konkani group](/wiki/Marathi-Konkani_languages "Marathi-Konkani languages") of the southern Indo-Aryan languages.[[31]](#cite_note-35) It is inflexive, and less distant from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") as compared to other modern [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"). Linguists describe Konkani as a fusion of a variety of [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit") vernacular languages. This could be attributed to the confluence of immigrants that the Konkan coast has witnessed over the years.[[32]](#cite_note-paisaci-36)

## Names and their etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Names and their etymology")]

It is quite possible that Old Konkani was just referred to as *Prakrit* by its speakers.[[33]](#cite_note-pak-37) Reference to the name *Konkani* is not found in literature prior to the 13th century. The first reference of the name *Konkani* is in "[Abhanga](/wiki/Abhanga "Abhanga") 263" of the 13th century [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu_religion "Hindu religion") Marathi saint poet, [Namadeva](/wiki/Namadeva "Namadeva") (1270–1350).[[34]](#cite_note-nam-38) Konkani has been known by a variety of names: *Canarim, Concanim, Gomantaki, Bramana,* and *Goani*. Learned Marathi speakers tend to call it *Gomantaki*.[[35]](#cite_note-39)

Konkani was commonly referred to as *Língua Canarim* by the Portuguese[[36]](#cite_note-rai-40) and *Língua Brahmana* by Catholic missionaries.[[36]](#cite_note-rai-40) The Portuguese later started referring to Konkani as *Língua Concanim*.[[36]](#cite_note-rai-40) The name *Canarim* or *Língua Canarim*, which is how the 16th century European [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") [Thomas Stephens](/wiki/Thomas_Stephens_(Jesuit) "Thomas Stephens (Jesuit)") refers to it in the title of his famous work *[Arte da lingoa Canarim](/wiki/Arte_da_lingoa_Canarim "Arte da lingoa Canarim")*, has always been intriguing. It is possible that the term is derived from the Persian word for coast, *kinara*; if so, it would mean "the language of the coast". The problem is that this term overlaps with *Kanarese* or [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada").[[37]](#cite_note-41)[[38]](#cite_note-42) All the European authors, however, recognised two forms of the language in Goa: the [plebeian](/wiki/Plebeian "Plebeian"), called *Canarim*, and the more regular (used by the educated classes), called *Língua Canarim Brâmana* or simply *Brâmana de Goa*. The latter was the preferred choice of the Europeans, and also of other castes, for writing, sermons, and religious purposes.[[39]](#cite_note-43)

There are different views as to the origin of the word Konkan and hence Konkani:

* V. P. Chavan states that the etymology of Konkan and hence Konkani is derived from the Kannada word *konku* meaning '*uneven ground*'. The Kannada origin suggests that Konkana might have included Kannada territory and '*uneven ground*' suggests the hilly nature of the territory.[[40]](#cite_note-44) *Konku* in Kannada also means that which is '*not straight'* and is '*crooked'*.[[41]](#cite_note-45)
* The word Konkan comes from the Kukkana ([Kokna](/wiki/Kokna "Kokna")) tribe, who were the original inhabitants of the land where Konkani originated.[[42]](#cite_note-manohar-46)
* According to some texts of [Puranas](/wiki/Purana "Purana"), [Parashurama](/wiki/Parashurama "Parashurama") shot his arrow into the sea and commanded the Sea God to recede up to the point where his arrow landed. The new piece of land thus recovered came to be known as [Konkan](/wiki/Konkan "Konkan") meaning *piece of earth* or *corner of earth*, *kōṇa* (corner) + *kaṇa* (piece). This legend is mentioned in [Sahyadrikhanda](/wiki/Sahyadrikhanda "Sahyadrikhanda") of the [Skanda Purana](/wiki/Skanda_Purana "Skanda Purana").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Proposed substrate influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Proposed substrate influences")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Postscript-viewer-blue.svg) | This section **needs attention from an expert in linguistics, languages or India**. The specific problem is: **The Kurukh and Oraon tribes speak Dravidian languages, not Austroasiatic.** [WikiProject Linguistics](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Linguistics "Wikipedia:WikiProject Linguistics"), [WikiProject Languages](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Languages "Wikipedia:WikiProject Languages") or [WikiProject India](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_India "Wikipedia:WikiProject India") may be able to help recruit an expert. *(October 2017)* |

The [substratum](/wiki/Substrata_(linguistics) "Substrata (linguistics)") of the Konkani language lies in the speech of [Austroasiatic](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages "Austroasiatic languages") tribes that may have once inhabited the region.[[43]](#cite_note-singh-47) According to the [Indian Anthropological Society](/wiki/Indian_Anthropological_Society "Indian Anthropological Society"), these Australoid tribes speaking Austro-Asiatic or Munda languages who once inhabited Konkan, migrated to Northern India ([Chota Nagpur Plateau](/wiki/Chota_Nagpur_Plateau "Chota Nagpur Plateau"), [Mirzapur](/wiki/Mirzapur "Mirzapur")) and are not found in Konkan any more.[[44]](#cite_note-IAS-48)[[45]](#cite_note-enthoven-49) Olivinho Gomes in his essay "Medieval Konkani Literature" also mentions the [Mundari](/wiki/Mundari_language "Mundari language") substratum.[[46]](#cite_note-others-50) Goan Indologist Anant Shenvi Dhume identified many Austro-Asiatic [Munda](/wiki/Munda_languages "Munda languages") words in Konkani, like *mund*, *mundkar*, *dhumak*, *goem-bab*.[[47]](#cite_note-dhume-51) This substratum is very prominent in Konkani.[[48]](#cite_note-mudns-52)

The grammatical influence of the Dravidian languages on the structure and syntax of Indo-Aryan languages is difficult to fathom. Some linguists explain this anomaly by arguing that Middle Indo-Aryan and New Indo-Aryan were built on a Dravidian [substratum](/wiki/Substrata_(linguistics) "Substrata (linguistics)").[[49]](#cite_note-53)
Some examples of Konkani words of Dravidian origin are: *naall* ([coconut](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut")), *madval* (washerman), *choru* (cooked rice) and *mulo* ([radish](/wiki/Radish "Radish")).[[50]](#cite_note-54) Linguists also suggest that the substratum of Marathi and Konkani is more closely related to Dravidian Kannada.[[51]](#cite_note-subkan-55)[[52]](#cite_note-56)

### Prehistory and early development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Prehistory and early development")]

Migrations of Indo-Aryan vernacular speakers have occurred throughout the history of the Indian west coast. Around 1100-700 BC the first wave of Indo-Aryans dialect speakers might have occurred, with the second wave appearing around 700-500 BC.[[47]](#cite_note-dhume-51) Many spoke [old Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Old_Indo-Aryan#Old_Indic "Old Indo-Aryan") vernacular languages, which may be loosely related to [Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit"); others still spoke Dravidian and Desi dialects. Thus the ancient Konkani Prakrit was born as a confluence of the Indo-Aryan dialects while accepting many words from Dravidian speech. Some linguists assume [Shauraseni](/wiki/Shauraseni "Shauraseni") to be its progenitor whereas some call it [Paisaci](/wiki/Paisaci "Paisaci"). The influence of Paisachi over Konkani can be proved in the findings of Taraporewala, who in his book *Elements of Science of Languages* (Calcutta University) ascertained that Konkani showed many [Dardic](/wiki/Dardic_languages "Dardic languages") features that are found in present-day [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language").[[32]](#cite_note-paisaci-36) Thus, the archaic form of old Konkani is referred to as Paishachi by some linguists.[[42]](#cite_note-manohar-46) This progenitor of Konkani (or Paishachi Apabhramsha) has preserved an older form of phonetic and grammatic development, showing a great variety of verbal forms found in Sanskrit and a large number of grammatical forms that are not found in Marathi. (Examples of this are found in many works like *[Dnyaneshwari](/wiki/Dnyaneshwari "Dnyaneshwari")*, and *Leela Charitra*.)[[53]](#cite_note-panic-57) Konkani thus developed with overall Sanskrit complexity and grammatical structure, which eventually developed into a lexical fund of its own.[[53]](#cite_note-panic-57) The second wave of Indo-Aryans is believed to have been accompanied by [Dravidians](/wiki/Dravidians "Dravidians") from the Deccan plateau.[[47]](#cite_note-dhume-51) Paishachi is also considered to be an Aryan language spoken by Dravidians.[[54]](#cite_note-pishacha-58)

Goa and Konkan were ruled by the [Konkan Mauryas](/wiki/Maurya "Maurya") and the [Bhojas](/wiki/Bhojas_of_Goa "Bhojas of Goa"); as a result, numerous migrations occurred from north, east and western India. Immigrants spoke various vernacular languages, which led to a mixture of features of Eastern and Western Prakrit. It was later substantially influenced by Magadhi Prakrit.[[55]](#cite_note-auto-59) The overtones of [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali")[[53]](#cite_note-panic-57) (the liturgical language of the Buddhists) also played a very important role in the development of Konkani Apabhramsha grammar and vocabulary.[[56]](#cite_note-gom2-60) A major number of linguistic innovations in Konkani are shared with [Eastern Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Eastern_Indo-Aryan_languages "Eastern Indo-Aryan languages") like [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") and [Oriya](/wiki/Oriya_language "Oriya language"), which have their roots in Magadhi.[[57]](#cite_note-east-61)

[Maharashtri Prakrit](/wiki/Maharashtri_Prakrit "Maharashtri Prakrit") is the ancestor of [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language") and Konkani,[[58]](#cite_note-62) it was the official language of the Satavahana Empire that ruled Goa and Konkan in the early centuries of the Common Era. Under the patronage of the Satavahana Empire, Maharashtri became the most widespread Prakrit of its time. Studying early Maharashtri compilations, many linguists have called Konkani "the first-born daughter of Maharashtri".[[59]](#cite_note-pani-63) This old language that was prevalent contemporary to old Marathi is found to be distinct from its counterpart.[[59]](#cite_note-pani-63)

The Sauraseni influence on Konkani is not as prominent as that of Maharashtri. Very few Konkani words are found to follow the Sauraseni pattern. Konkani forms are rather more akin to Pali than the corresponding Sauraseni forms.[[60]](#cite_note-auto1-64) The major Sauraseni influence on Konkani is the *ao* sound found at the end of many nouns in Sauraseni, which becomes *o* or *u* in Konkani.[[61]](#cite_note-auto2-65) Examples include: *dando*, *suno*, *raakhano*, *dukh*, *rukhu*, *manisu* (from Prakrit), *dandao*, *sunnao*, *rakkhakao*, *dukkhao*, *vukkhao*, *vrukkhao*, and *mannisso*. Another example could be the sound of *ण* at the beginning of words; it is still retained in many Konkani words of archaic Shauraseni origin, such as णव (nine). Archaic Konkani born out of [Shauraseni](/wiki/Shauraseni "Shauraseni") vernacular Prakrit at the earlier stage of the evolution (and later [Maharashtri](/wiki/Maharashtri "Maharashtri") Prakrit), was commonly spoken until 875 AD, and at its later phase ultimately developed into [Apabhramsha](/wiki/Apabhra%E1%B9%83%C5%9Ba "Apabhraṃśa"), which could be called a predecessor of old Konkani.[[56]](#cite_note-gom2-60)

Although most of the stone inscriptions and copper plates found in Goa (and other parts of Konkan) from the 2nd century BC to the 10th century AD are in Prakrit-influenced Sanskrit (mostly written in early Brahmi and archaic Dravidian Brahmi), most of the places, grants, agricultural-related terms, and names of some people are in Konkani. This suggests that Konkani was spoken in Goa and Konkan.[[62]](#cite_note-dhume2-66)

Though it belongs to the Indo-Aryan group, Konkani was influenced by a language of the Dravidian family. A branch of the [Kadambas](/wiki/Kadambas "Kadambas"), who ruled Goa for a long period, had their roots in [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"). Konkani was never used for official purposes.[[63]](#cite_note-mitra-67) Another reason Kannada influenced Konkani was the proximity of original Konkani-speaking territories to Karnataka.[[64]](#cite_note-manohar1-68) Old Konkani documents show considerable Kannada influence on grammar as well as vocabulary. Like southern Dravidian languages, Konkani has [prothetic glides](/wiki/Prosthesis_(linguistics) "Prosthesis (linguistics)") *y-* and *w-*.[[65]](#cite_note-konk-69) The Kannada influence is more evident in Konkani syntax. The question markers in *yes/no* questions and the negative marker are sentence final.[[65]](#cite_note-konk-69) [Copula deletion](/wiki/Copula_(linguistics) "Copula (linguistics)") in Konkani is remarkably similar to Kannada.[[65]](#cite_note-konk-69) [Phrasal verbs](/wiki/Phrasal_verbs "Phrasal verbs") are not so commonly used in Indo-Aryan languages; however, Konkani spoken in Dravidian regions has borrowed numerous phrasal verb patterns.[[66]](#cite_note-indo-70)

The Kols, Kharwas, Yadavas, and [Lothal](/wiki/Lothal "Lothal") migrants all settled in Goa during the pre-historic period and later. *Chavada*, a tribe of warriors (also now known as *Chaddi* or *[Chaddo](/wiki/Christian_Cxatria#Origins "Christian Cxatria")*), migrated to Goa from [Saurashtra](/wiki/Saurashtra_(region) "Saurashtra (region)"), during the 7th and 8th century AD, after their kingdom was destroyed by the [Arabs](/wiki/Arab "Arab") in 740.[[67]](#cite_note-gazat-71) Royal matrimonial relationships between the two states, as well as trade relationships, had a major influence on Goan society. Many of these groups spoke different Nagar [Apabhramsha](/wiki/Apabhramsha "Apabhramsha") dialects, which could be seen as precursors of modern Gujarati.

* Konkani and Gujarati have many words in common, not found in Marathi.[[68]](#cite_note-guj-72)
* The Konkani *O* (as opposed to the Marathi *A*, which is of different Prakrit origin), is similar to that in Gujarati.[[68]](#cite_note-guj-72)
* The case terminations in Konkani, *lo*, *li*, and *le*, and the Gujarati *no*, *ni*, and *ne* have the same Prakrit roots.[[68]](#cite_note-guj-72)
* In both languages the [present indicatives](/wiki/Present_indicative "Present indicative") have no gender, unlike Marathi.[[68]](#cite_note-guj-72)

### Early

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early")]

An inscription at the foot of the colossal Jain monolith [Bahubali](/wiki/Bahubali "Bahubali") (The word *gomateshvara* apparently comes from Konkani **gomaṭo** which means "beautiful" or "handsome" and *īśvara* "lord".[[69]](#cite_note-gomto-73)) at [Shravanabelagola](/wiki/Shravanabelagola "Shravanabelagola") of 981 CE reads, in a variant of Nāgarī:[[70]](#cite_note-74)

> "śrīcāvuṇḍarājē̃ kara viyālē̃, śrīgaṅgārājē̃ suttālē̃ kara viyālē̃" ([Chavundaraya](/wiki/Chavundaraya "Chavundaraya") got it done, Gangaraya got the surroundings done).[[note 4]](#cite_note-75)[[note 5]](#cite_note-76)

The language of these lines is Konkani according to S.B. Kulkarni (former head of Department of Marathi, [Nagpur University](/wiki/Nagpur_University "Nagpur University")) and Jose Pereira (former professor, [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University"), USA).[[71]](#cite_note-77)

Another inscription in [Nāgarī](/wiki/N%C4%81gar%C4%AB_script "Nāgarī script"), of [Shilahara](/wiki/Shilahara "Shilahara") King Aparaditya II of the year 1187 AD in [Parel](/wiki/Parel "Parel") reportedly contains Marathi according to former claims.[[72]](#cite_note-78) A recent 2024 study has found that the last two lines of the Parel inscription, also called, the Mahavali inscription because it was discovered at Mahavali in Kurla, Bombay (Mumbai), is in the Konkani language.[[73]](#cite_note-79)

Many stone and copper-plate inscriptions found in Goa and Konkan are written in Konkani. The grammar and the base of such texts is in Konkani, whereas very few verbs are in Marathi.[[74]](#cite_note-sal-80) Copper plates found in Ponda dating back to the early 13th century, and from [Quepem](/wiki/Quepem "Quepem") in the early 14th century, have been written in [Goykanadi](/wiki/Goykanadi "Goykanadi").[[46]](#cite_note-others-50) One such stone inscription or *shilalekh* (written [Nāgarī](/wiki/N%C4%81gar%C4%AB_script "Nāgarī script")) is found at the Nageshi temple in Goa (dating back to the year 1463 AD). It mentions that the (then) ruler of Goa, Devaraja Gominam, had gifted land to the [Nagueshi](/wiki/Nagueshi "Nagueshi") Maharudra temple when Nanjanna Gosavi was the religious head or *Pratihasta* of the state. It mentions words like, *kullgga*, *kulaagra*, *naralel*, *tambavem*, and *tilel*.[[75]](#cite_note-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Nagueshi_Inscription_1413AD.jpg/250px-Nagueshi_Inscription_1413AD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagueshi_Inscription_1413AD.jpg)

Konkani Inscription with 'Maee Shenvi' of 1413 AD, [Nagueshi](/wiki/Nagueshi "Nagueshi"), Goa.

A piece of hymn dedicated to Lord [Narayana](/wiki/Narayana "Narayana") attributed to the 12th century AD says:

> *"jaṇẽ rasataḷavāntũ matsyarūpē̃ vēda āṇiyēlē̃. manuśivāka vāṇiyēlē̃. to saṁsārasāgara tāraṇu. mōhō to rākho nārāyāṇu".* (The one who brought the [Vedas](/wiki/Veda "Veda") up from the ocean in the form of a fish, from the bottoms of the water and offered it to [Manu](/wiki/Manu_(Hinduism) "Manu (Hinduism)"), he is the one Saviour of the world, that is Narayana my God.).

A hymn from the later 16th century goes

> *vaikuṇṭhācē̃ jhāḍa tu gē phaḷa amṛtācē̃, jīvita rākhilē̃ tuvē̃ manasakuḷācē̃.*[[76]](#cite_note-Sardesaya-82)

Early Konkani was marked by the use of pronouns like *dzo*, *jī*, and *jẽ*. These are replaced in contemporary Konkani by *koṇa*. The conjunctions *yedō* and *tedō* ("when" and "then") which were used in early Konkani are no longer in use.[[77]](#cite_note-Maffei-83) The use of *-viyalẽ* has been replaced by *-aylẽ*. The pronoun *moho*, which is similar to the Brajbhasha word *mōhē* has been replaced by *mākā*.

### Medieval

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Medieval")]

This era was marked by several invasions of Goa and subsequent exodus of some Konkani families to [Canara](/wiki/Kanara "Kanara") (today's coastal Karnataka), and Cochin.

* Exodus (between 1312 and 1327) when General [Malik Kafur](/wiki/Malik_Kafur "Malik Kafur") of the [Delhi Sultans](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate"), [Alauddin Khalji](/wiki/Alauddin_Khalji "Alauddin Khalji"), and [Muhammed bin Tughlaq](/wiki/Muhammad_bin_Tughluq "Muhammad bin Tughluq") destroyed [Govepuri](/wiki/Govapuri "Govapuri") and the [Kadambas](/wiki/Kadambas "Kadambas")
* Exodus subsequent to 1470 when the [Bahmani](/wiki/Bahmani "Bahmani") kingdom captured Goa, and subsequent capture in 1492 by Sultan [Yusuf Adil Shah](/wiki/Yusuf_Adil_Shah "Yusuf Adil Shah") of [Bijapur](/wiki/Bijapur_Sultanate "Bijapur Sultanate")
* Exodus of converted Muslims to Bijapur-held territory due to the [Portuguese conquest of Goa](/wiki/Portuguese_conquest_of_Goa "Portuguese conquest of Goa") in 1510
* Migration of Hindu converts to [Canara](/wiki/Canara "Canara") in South India after the [Christianisation of Goa](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Goa "Christianisation of Goa"), the subsequent [Goa Inquisition](/wiki/Goa_Inquisition "Goa Inquisition"), and the [Sackings of Goa and Bombay-Bassein](/wiki/Mahratta_Sackings_of_Goa_and_Bombay-Bassein "Mahratta Sackings of Goa and Bombay-Bassein").

These events caused the Konkani language to develop into multiple dialects with multiple scripts. The exodus to coastal Karnataka and Kerala required Konkani speakers in these regions to learn the local languages. This caused penetration of local words into the dialects of Konkani spoken by these speakers. Examples include *dār* (door) giving way to the word *bāgil*. Also, the phoneme "a" in the Salcette dialect was replaced by the phoneme "o".

Other Konkani communities came into being with their own dialects of Konkani. The [Konkani Muslim](/wiki/Konkani_Muslims "Konkani Muslims") communities of [Ratnagiri](/wiki/Ratnagiri "Ratnagiri") and [Coastal Karnataka](/wiki/Coastal_Karnataka "Coastal Karnataka") came about due to a mixture of intermarriages of Arab seafarers, Middle East businessman, Britishes and locals as well as conversions of Hindus to Islam.[[78]](#cite_note-84) Another migrant community that picked up Konkani are the [Siddis](/wiki/Siddis "Siddis"), who are descended from [Bantu peoples](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") from South East Africa that were brought to the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") as slaves.[[79]](#cite_note-Shah-85)

### Contemporary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Contemporary")]

Contemporary Konkani is written in Devanagari, Kannada, Malayalam, Persian, and Roman scripts. It is written by speakers in their native dialects. The Goan Antruz dialect in the Devanagari script has been promulgated as Standard Konkani.

## Revitalisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Revitalisation")]

See also: [Konkani Language Agitation](/wiki/Konkani_Language_Agitation "Konkani Language Agitation")

Konkani language was in decline, due to the use of Portuguese as the official and social language among the Christians, the predominance of Marathi over Konkani among Hindus, and the Konkani Christian-Hindu divide. Seeing this, Vaman Raghunath Varde Valaulikar set about on a mission to unite all Konkanis, Hindus as well as Christians, regardless of caste or religion. He saw this movement not just as a nationalistic movement against Portuguese rule, but also against the pre-eminence of Marathi over Konkani. Almost single-handedly he crusaded, writing a number of works in Konkani. He is regarded as the pioneer of modern Konkani literature and affectionately remembered as [Shenoi Goembab](/wiki/Shenoi_Goembab "Shenoi Goembab").[[80]](#cite_note-goanews-86) His death anniversary, 9 April, is celebrated as World Konkani Day (Vishwa Konkani Dis).[[81]](#cite_note-87)

Madhav Manjunath Shanbhag, an advocate by profession from Karwar, who with a few like-minded companions travelled throughout all the Konkani speaking areas, sought to unite the fragmented Konkani community under the banner of "one language, one script, one literature". He succeeded in organising the first [All India Konkani Parishad](/wiki/All_India_Konkani_Parishad "All India Konkani Parishad") in Karwar in 1939.[[82]](#cite_note-88) Successive *adhiveshans* of All India Konkani Parishad were held at various places in subsequent years. At least 29 *adhiveshans* of All India Konkani Parishad have been held since.

### Post-independence period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Post-independence period")]

Following India's independence and its subsequent [annexation of Goa](/wiki/Annexation_of_Goa "Annexation of Goa") in 1961, Goa was absorbed into the Indian Union as a Union Territory, directly under central administration.

However, with the reorganisation of states along linguistic lines, and growing calls from Maharashtra, as well as Marathis in Goa for the merger of Goa into Maharashtra, an intense debate was started in Goa. The main issues discussed were the status of Konkani as an independent language and Goa's future as a part of Maharashtra or as an independent state. The [Goa Opinion Poll](/wiki/Goa_Opinion_Poll "Goa Opinion Poll"), a plebiscite, retained Goa as an independent state in 1967.[[80]](#cite_note-goanews-86) However, English, Hindi, and Marathi continued to be the preferred languages for official communication, while Konkani was sidelined.[[7]](#cite_note-goacom2049-9)

### Recognition as an independent language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Recognition as an independent language")]

With the continued insistence of some Marathis that Konkani was a dialect of Marathi and not an independent language, the matter was finally placed before the [Sahitya Akademi](/wiki/Sahitya_Akademi "Sahitya Akademi"). [Suniti Kumar Chatterji](/wiki/Suniti_Kumar_Chatterji "Suniti Kumar Chatterji"), the president of the Akademi appointed a committee of linguistic experts to settle the dispute. On 26 February 1975, the committee came to the conclusion that Konkani was indeed an independent and literary language, classified as an Indo-European language, which in its present state was heavily influenced by the Portuguese language.

### Official language status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Official language status")]

All this did not change anything in Goa. Finally, fed up with the delay, Konkani activists launched an agitation in 1986, demanding official status for Konkani. The agitation turned violent in various places, resulting in the death of six agitators from the Catholic community: [Floriano Vaz](/wiki/Floriano_Vaz "Floriano Vaz") from Gogol Margao, Aldrin Fernandes, Mathew Faria, C. J. Dias, John Fernandes, and Joaquim Pereira, all from [Agaçaim](/wiki/Aga%C3%A7aim "Agaçaim"). Finally, on 4 February 1987, the [Goa Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Goa_Legislative_Assembly "Goa Legislative Assembly") passed the Official Language Bill, making Konkani the official language of Goa.[[7]](#cite_note-goacom2049-9)

Konkani was included in the [Eighth Schedule to the Constitution of India](/wiki/Eighth_Schedule_to_the_Constitution_of_India "Eighth Schedule to the Constitution of India") as per the [Seventy-First Amendment](/wiki/Seventy-first_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_India "Seventy-first Amendment of the Constitution of India") on 20 August 1992, adding it to the list of official languages.

## Geographical distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geographical distribution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Konkpic.JPG/250px-Konkpic.JPG)](/wiki/File:Konkpic.JPG)

Modern day Goan Konkani in [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari")

The Konkani language originated and is spoken widely in the western coastal region of India known as [Konkan](/wiki/Konkan "Konkan"). The native lands historically inhabited by Konkani people include the [Konkan division](/wiki/Konkan_division "Konkan division") of [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"), the state of [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa") and the territory of [Daman](/wiki/Daman_District,_India "Daman District, India"), the [Uttara Kannada](/wiki/Uttara_Kannada "Uttara Kannada"), [Udupi](/wiki/Udupi_district "Udupi district") & [Dakshina Kannada](/wiki/Dakshina_Kannada "Dakshina Kannada") districts of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), [belagavi](/wiki/Belagavi "Belagavi"), [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore"), and [Bengaluru](/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru") along with many districts in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") such as [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod "Kasaragod"), [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi"), [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha "Alappuzha"), [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram") and [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam"). All of the regions and areas have developed distinct [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect"), [pronunciation](/wiki/Pronunciation "Pronunciation") and [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose") styles, vocabulary, tone, and sometimes, significant differences in grammar.[[83]](#cite_note-kurzon-89)

According to the 2001 estimates of the Census Department of India, there were 2,489,016 Konkani speakers in India.[[84]](#cite_note-90) The Census Department of India, 2011 figures put the number of Konkani speakers in India as 2,256,502 making up 0.19% of India's population. Out of these, 788,294 were in [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), 964,305 in [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"),[[85]](#cite_note-languagedata2011-91) 399,255 in [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"), and 69,449 in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"). It ranks 19th on the List of Scheduled Languages by strength. The number of Konkani speakers in India fell by 9.34% in the decade 2001–2011. It is the only scheduled language apart from Urdu to have a negative growth rate in the decade. A very large number of Konkanis live outside India, either as expatriates ([NRIs](/wiki/Non-resident_Indian_and_person_of_Indian_origin "Non-resident Indian and person of Indian origin")) with [work visas](/wiki/Work_visa "Work visa") or as naturalised citizens and permanent residents of other host countries ([immigrants](/wiki/Immigrants "Immigrants")). Determining their numbers is difficult since Konkani is a [minority language](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") that is very often not recognised by censuses and surveys of various government agencies and NGOs catering to Indians abroad.

During the days of [Portuguese Goa](/wiki/Portuguese_Goa "Portuguese Goa") and [British rule](/wiki/British_rule "British rule") in [Pre-Partition India](/wiki/Pre-Partition_India "Pre-Partition India") many Goans and non-Goan Konkani people went to foreign countries as economic migrants to the colonies of [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") and [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") respectively, and also to the Pakistan of [Pre-Partition India](/wiki/Pre-Partition_India "Pre-Partition India"). The migratory trend has continued well into the post-colonial era and a significant number of Konkani people are found in [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), the [Persian Gulf countries](/wiki/Persian_Gulf_countries "Persian Gulf countries"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), and the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") and the rest of the [Anglosphere](/wiki/Anglosphere "Anglosphere"). Many families still continue to speak different Konkani dialects that their ancestors spoke, which are now highly influenced by the languages of the dominant majority.

## Status and issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Status and issues")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **contains statements that are [anachronistic](/wiki/Anachronistic "Anachronistic")**. Please help [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit) by updating, clarifying, or removing such statements. *(October 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The Konkani language has been in danger of dying out over the years for many of the following reasons:

1. The fragmentation of Konkani into various, sometimes mutually unintelligible, dialects.
2. The dominance of [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language") and the large degree of [bilingualism](/wiki/Bilingualism "Bilingualism") of Konkani Hindus in [Goa state](/wiki/Goa_state "Goa state"), the union territory of [Damaon](/wiki/Daman_and_Diu "Daman and Diu") and the [Konkan division](/wiki/Konkan_division "Konkan division") of Maharashtra.[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
3. Progressive inroads made by [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") into the [Konkani Muslim](/wiki/Konkani_Muslim "Konkani Muslim") community.[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]
4. Mutual animosity among various religious sects and caste groups; including a secondary status of Konkani culture to religion.
5. The migration of Konkanis to various parts of India and around the world.
6. The lack of opportunities to study Konkani in schools and colleges. Even until recently there were few Konkani schools in Goa. Populations outside the native Konkani areas have absolutely no access to Konkani language studies, literature and media.
7. The preference among Konkani parents to speak to their children in *potaachi bhaas* (language of the stomach) over *maai bhaas* ([mother tongue](/wiki/Mother_tongue "Mother tongue")). They sometimes speak primarily in English to help their children gain a grip on English in schools.[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1)

As such, Konkani has been classified in the "Unsafe" Category of the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger](/wiki/Atlas_of_the_World%27s_Languages_in_Danger "Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger") since 2009.[[86]](#cite_note-92)

Efforts have been made to stop this downward trend of usage of Konkani, starting with [Shenoi Goembab](/wiki/Shenoi_Goembab "Shenoi Goembab")'s efforts to revive Konkani. The recognition granted by [Sahitya Akademi](/wiki/Sahitya_Akademi "Sahitya Akademi") to Konkani and the institution of an annual award for Konkani literature has helped to a certain extent.

Some organisations, such as the [Konkan Daiz Yatra](/w/index.php?title=Konkan_Daiz_Yatra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Konkan Daiz Yatra (page does not exist)") organised by Konkani Bhasha Mandal, [World Konkani Centre](/wiki/World_Konkani_Centre "World Konkani Centre") and the newer Vishwa Konkani Parishad have laid great stress on uniting all factions of Konkanis.

### Opposition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Opposition")]

#### Marathi dispute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Marathi dispute")]

Main article: [Konkani language agitation](/wiki/Konkani_language_agitation "Konkani language agitation")

José Pereira, in his 1971 work *Konkani – A Language: A History of the Konkani Marathi Controversy*, pointed to an essay on Indian languages written by John Leyden in 1807, wherein Konkani is called a "dialect of Maharashtra" as an origin of the language controversy.[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1)

Another linguist to whom this theory is attributed is [Grierson](/wiki/George_Abraham_Grierson "George Abraham Grierson"). Grierson's work on the languages of India, *the Linguistic Survey of India*, was regarded as an important reference by other linguists. In his book, Grierson had distinguished between the Konkani spoken in coastal Maharashtra (then, part of Bombay) and the Konkani spoken in Goa as two different languages. He regarded the Konkani spoken in coastal Maharashtra as a dialect of Marathi and not as a dialect of Goan Konkani itself. In his opinion, Goan Konkani was also considered a dialect of Marathi because the religious literature used by the Hindus in Goa was not in Konkani itself, but in Marathi.

S. M. Katre's 1966 work, *The Formation of Konkani*, which utilised the instruments of modern historical and comparative linguistics across six typical Konkani dialects, showed the formation of Konkani to be distinct from that of Marathi.[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1)[[87]](#cite_note-languageinindia.com-93) [Shenoi Goembab](/wiki/Shenoi_Goembab "Shenoi Goembab"), who played a pivotal role in the Konkani revival movement, rallied against the pre-eminence of Marathi over Konkani amongst Hindus and Portuguese amongst Christians.

Goa's accession to India in 1961 came at a time when Indian states were being reorganised along linguistic lines. There were demands to merge Goa with Maharashtra. This was because Goa had a sizeable population of Marathi speakers and Konkani was also considered to be a dialect of Marathi by many. Konkani Goans were opposed to the move. The status of Konkani as an independent language or as a dialect of Marathi had a great political bearing on Goa's merger, which was settled by a plebiscite in 1967 (the [Goa Opinion Poll](/wiki/Goa_Opinion_Poll "Goa Opinion Poll")).[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1)

The [Sahitya Akademi](/wiki/Sahitya_Akademi "Sahitya Akademi") (a prominent literary organisation in India) recognised it as an independent language in 1975, and subsequently Konkani (in [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") script) was made the official language of Goa in 1987.

### Karnataka

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Karnataka")]

MLC [Ivan D'Souza](/wiki/Ivan_D%27Souza "Ivan D'Souza") attempted to speak in Konkani at the [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") state's Legislative Council, but was urged not to by the Chairman D H Shankaramurthy as most of the audience did not know Konkani. Even though Mr D'Souza pleaded that Konkani was amongst the 22 official languages recognised by the Indian Constitution, he was not given permission to continue in Konkani.[[88]](#cite_note-D'Souza1-94)

Even though there are substantial Konkani [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics") in Bengaluru, efforts to celebrate Holy Mass in Konkani have met with opposition by [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_people "Kannada people") "activists". Konkani Holy Masses has been held in the Sabbhavana and Saccidananda chapels of the Carmelite and Capuchin Fathers respectively, in [Yeswanthpur](/wiki/Yeswanthpur "Yeswanthpur") and [Rajajinagar](/wiki/Rajajinagar "Rajajinagar"), Bangalore. These services are under threat from Kannada groups who do not want [church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") to be held in any language other than [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_language "Kannada language"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] even though Kannada Catholics constitute only 30% of the Catholic population in the Archdiocese. Konkani activists and associations have been demanding Konkani language mass and services for a long time.[[89]](#cite_note-Mass1-95) It is still the official language of the [Mangalore Diocese](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Mangalore "Roman Catholic Diocese of Mangalore").[[90]](#cite_note-Saldanha-Shet-96)

### Multilingualism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Multilingualism")]

According to the Census Department of India, Konkani speakers show a very high degree of [multilingualism](/wiki/Multilingualism "Multilingualism"). In the 1991 census, as compared to the national average of 19.44% for bilingualism and 7.26% for trilingualism, Konkani speakers scored 74.20% and 44.68% respectively. This makes the Konkans the most multilingual community of India.

This has been due to the fact that in most areas where Konkans have settled, they seldom form a majority of the population and have to interact with others in the local tongue. Another reason for bilingualism has been the lack of schools teaching Konkani as a primary or secondary language.

The bilingualism of Konkanis with Marathi in Daman Goa and Maharashtra has been a source of great discontent because it has led to the belief that Konkani is a dialect of Marathi[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1)[[87]](#cite_note-languageinindia.com-93) and hence has no bearing on the future of Goa.

### Scripts and dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Scripts and dialects")]

Main article: [Konkani alphabets](/wiki/Konkani_alphabets "Konkani alphabets")

The problems posed by multiple scripts and varying dialects have come as an impediment in the efforts to unite Konkani people. The Goa state's decision to use [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") as the official script and the Antruz dialect has been met with opposition both within Goa and outside it.[[7]](#cite_note-goacom2049-9) Critics contend that the Antruz dialect is unintelligible to most Goans, let alone other Konkani people outside Goa, and that Devanagari is used very little as compared to [Romi Konkani](/wiki/Romi_Konkani "Romi Konkani") in Goa or Konkani in the [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada") script.[[7]](#cite_note-goacom2049-9) Prominent among the critics are Konkani Christians in Goa, who were at the forefront of the Konkani agitation in 1986–87 and have for a long time used the Roman script, including producing literature in Roman script. They demanded Roman script be given equal status to Devanagari.[[91]](#cite_note-97)

In Karnataka, which has the largest number of Konkani speakers after Goa, leading organisations and activists have similarly demanded that Kannada script be made the medium of instruction for Konkani in local schools instead of Devanagari.[[92]](#cite_note-98) The government of Karnataka has given its approval for teaching of Konkani as an optional third language from 6th to 10th standard students either in Kannada or Devanagari scripts.[[93]](#cite_note-99)

### Outside India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Outside India")]

Emigration has also brought the Konkani language to foreign countries, such as the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") and [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya").

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Phonology")]

See also: [Konkani phonology](/wiki/Konkani_phonology "Konkani phonology")

The Konkani language has 16 basic vowels (excluding an equal number of long vowels), 36 consonants, 5 semi-vowels, 3 sibilants, 1 aspirate, and many [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"). Like the other [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"), it has both long and short vowels and syllables with long vowels may appear to be stressed. Different types of nasal vowels are a special feature of the Konkani language.[[94]](#cite_note-100)

* The [palatal](/wiki/Palatal_consonant "Palatal consonant") and [alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant") stops are [affricates](/wiki/Affricate "Affricate"). The palatal glides are truly palatal but otherwise the consonants in the palatal column are [alveopalatal](/wiki/Alveopalatal "Alveopalatal").[[95]](#cite_note-cardona-101)
* The [voiced/voiceless](/wiki/Voice_(phonetics) "Voice (phonetics)") contrasts are found only in the stops and affricates. The fricatives are all voiceless and the [sonorants](/wiki/Sonorants "Sonorants") are all voiced.[[95]](#cite_note-cardona-101)
* The initial vowel-syllable is shortened after the [aspirates](/wiki/Aspiration_(phonetics) "Aspiration (phonetics)") and [fricatives](/wiki/Fricative "Fricative"). Many speakers substitute unaspirated consonants for aspirates.[[95]](#cite_note-cardona-101)
* Aspirates in a non-initial position are rare and only occur in careful speech. [Palatalisation](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)")/non-palatisation is found in all [obstruents](/wiki/Obstruent "Obstruent"), except for palatal and alveolars. Where a palatalised alveolar is expected, a palatal is found instead. In the case of sonorants, only unaspirated consonants show this contrast, and among the glides only labeo-velar glides exhibit this. Vowels show a contrast between oral and nasal ones[[95]](#cite_note-cardona-101)

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Konkani grammar is similar to other Indo-Aryan languages. Notably, Konkani grammar is also influenced by Dravidian languages. It cannot be described as a [stress-timed language](/wiki/Stress-timed_language "Stress-timed language"), nor as a [tonal language](/wiki/Tonal_language "Tonal language").[[97]](#cite_note-D2_Konkani-104)

* Speech can be classified into any of the following parts:[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105)

1. *naam* ([noun](/wiki/Noun "Noun"))
2. *sarvanaam* ([pronoun](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun"))
3. *visheshan* ([adjective](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective"))
4. *kriyapad* ([verb](/wiki/Verb "Verb"))
5. *kriyavisheshana* ([adverb](/wiki/Adverb "Adverb"))
6. *ubhayanvayi avyaya*
7. *shabdayogi avyaya*
8. *kevalaprayogi avyaya* ([interjection](/wiki/Interjection "Interjection"))

Like most of the Indo-Aryan languages, Konkani is an [SOV](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93object%E2%80%93verb "Subject–object–verb") language, meaning among other things that not only is the verb found at the end of the clause but also [modifiers](/wiki/Modifiers "Modifiers") and complements tend to precede the head and [postpositions](/wiki/Postpositions "Postpositions") are far more common than [prepositions](/wiki/Prepositions "Prepositions"). In terms of syntax, Konkani is a *head-last* language, unlike English, which is an [SVO](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object") language.[[99]](#cite_note-kurzden-106)

* Almost all the verbs, adverbs, adjectives, and the *avyaya*s are either *tatsama* or *tadbhava*.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105)

### Verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Verbs")]

Verbs are either *tatsama* or *tadbhava*:[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105)

**Verbs and their roots:**

| Konkani verbs | Sanskrit/Prakrit Root | Translation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| वाच vaach (tatsama) | वच् vach | read |
| आफय, आपय aaphay, aapay (tatsama) | आव्हय् aavhay | call, summon |
| रांध raandh (tatsama) | रांध् raandh | cook |
| बरय baray (tadbhav) | वर्णय् varnay | write |
| व्हर vhar (tadbhav) | हर har | take away |
| भक bhak (tadbhav) | भक्ष् bhaksh | eat |
| हेड hedd (tadbhav) | अट् att | roam |
| ल्हेव lhev (tadbhav) | लेह् leh | lick |
| शीन sheen (tadbhav) | छिन्न chinna | cut |
| Source: *Koṅkaṇî Dhatukosh*[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105) | | | | |

* Present indefinite of the auxiliary is fused with [present participle](/wiki/Present_participle "Present participle") of the primary verb, and the auxiliary is partially dropped.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105) When the southern dialects came in contact with Dravidian languages this difference became more prominent in Karnataka and Kerala whereas Goan Konkani still retains the original form.

For example, "I eat" and "I am eating" sound similar in Goan Konkani, due to loss of auxiliary in colloquial speech. "Hāv khātā" corresponds to "I am eating". On the other hand, in Karnataka Konkani "hāv khātā" corresponds to "I eat", and "hāv khātoāsā" or "hāv khāter āsā" means "I am eating". However the word "jito" (living) is universal, "to jitoāsā" (he is living).

* Out of eight [grammatical cases](/wiki/Grammatical_cases "Grammatical cases"), Konkani has totally lost the [dative](/wiki/Dative_case "Dative case"), the [locative](/wiki/Locative_case "Locative case"), and the [ablative](/wiki/Ablative_case "Ablative case").[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105) It has partially lost the [accusative](/wiki/Accusative_case "Accusative case") and the [instrumental cases](/wiki/Instrumental_case "Instrumental case") too.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105) So the preserved cases are: the [nominative](/wiki/Nominative_case "Nominative case"), the [genitive](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case"), and the [vocative case](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case").[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105)

### Apabhramsha and metathesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Apabhramsha and metathesis")]

* Like Marathi and Gujarati, the Konkani language has three genders. During the Middle Ages, most of the Indo-Aryan languages lost their neuter gender, except Maharashtri, in which it is retained much more in Marathi than Konkani.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105) Gender in Konkani is purely grammatical and unconnected to gender.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105)

[Metathesis](/wiki/Metathesis_(linguistics) "Metathesis (linguistics)") is a characteristic of all the middle and modern Indo-Aryan languages including Konkani. Consider the Sanskrit word "स्नुषा" (daughter-in law). Here, the ष is dropped, and स्नु alone is utilised, स्नु-->स/नु and you get the word सुन (metathesis of *ukar*).[[100]](#cite_note-meta-107)

* Unlike Sanskrit, *[anusvara](/wiki/Anusvara "Anusvara")* has great importance in Konkani. A characteristic of [Middle Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Middle_Indo-Aryan "Middle Indo-Aryan") dialects, Konkani still retains the *anusvara* on the initial or final syllable.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105) Similarly *[visarga](/wiki/Visarga "Visarga")*, is totally lost and is assimilated with उ and/or ओ. For example, in Sanskrit दीपः becomes दिवो and दुःख becomes दुख.
* Konkani retains the pitch accent, which is a direct derivative of Vedic accent, which probably would account for "nasalism" in Konkani.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105) The "breathed" accent is retained in most of the *tatsama*s than the *tadbhava*s.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105) [Declension](/wiki/Declension "Declension") also affects the accent.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105)
* Konkani has lost its passive voice, and now the transitive verbs in their perfects are equivalent to passives.
* Konkani has rejected ऋ, ॠ, ऌ, ॡ, ष, and क्ष, which are assimilated with र, ख, ह, श and स.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105)
* Sanskrit compound letters are avoided in Konkani. For example, in Sanskrit द्वे, प्राय, गृहस्थ, उद्योत become बे, पिराय, गिरेस्त, and उज्जो respectively in Konkani.[[98]](#cite_note-jana-105)

## Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

The vocabulary from Konkani comes from a number of sources. The main source is Prakrits. So Sanskrit as a whole has played a very important part in Konkani vocabulary. Konkani vocabulary is made of *tatsama* (Sanskrit loanwords without change), *tadhbhava* (evolved Sanskrit words), *deshya* (indigenous words) and *antardeshya* (foreign words). Other sources of vocabulary are Arabic, Persian, and Turkish. Finally, Kannada, Marathi, and Portuguese have enriched its lexical content.[[99]](#cite_note-kurzden-106)

### Loanwords

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Loanwords")]

Since Goa was a major trade centre for visiting Arabs and Turks, many Arabic and Persian words infiltrated the Konkani language.[[64]](#cite_note-manohar1-68) A large number of Arabic and Persian words now form an integral part of Konkani vocabulary and are commonly used in day-to-day life; examples are *karz* (debt), *fakt* (only), *dusman* (enemy), and *barik* (thin).[[64]](#cite_note-manohar1-68) Single and compound words are found wherein the original meaning has been changed or distorted. Examples include *mustaiki* (from Arabic *mustaid*, meaning "ready"), and *kapan khairo* ("eater of one's own shroud", meaning "a miser").

Most of the old Konkani Hindu literature does not show any influence from Portuguese. Even the dialects spoken by the majority of Goan Hindus have a very limited Portuguese influence. On the other hand, dialects spoken by the Catholics from Goa (as well as the Canara to some extent) and their religious literature show a strong Portuguese influence. They contain a number of Portuguese lexical items, but these are almost all religious terms. Even in the context of religious terminology, the missionaries adapted native terms associated with Hindu religious concepts. (For example, *krupa* for grace, Y*amakunda* for hell, V*aikuntha* for paradise and so on). The syntax used by [Goan Catholics](/wiki/Goan_Catholics "Goan Catholics") in [their literature](/wiki/Literature_of_Goan_Catholics "Literature of Goan Catholics") shows a prominent Portuguese influence. As a result, many Portuguese loanwords are now commonly found in common Konkani speech.[[101]](#cite_note-porin-108)[[102]](#cite_note-109)
The Portuguese influence is also evident in the [Marathi–Konkani](/wiki/Marathi%E2%80%93Konkani_languages "Marathi–Konkani languages") spoken in the former Northern Konkan district, [Thane](/wiki/Thane "Thane") a variant of Konkani used by [Bombay East Indians](/wiki/Bombay_East_Indians "Bombay East Indians") Catholic community.

### Sanskritisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Sanskritisation")]

Konkani is not highly Sanskritised like [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language"), but still retains [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit") and [apabhramsa](/wiki/Apabhramsa "Apabhramsa") structures, verbal forms, and vocabulary. Though the Goan Hindu dialect is highly Prakritised, numerous Sanskrit [loanwords](/wiki/Loanwords "Loanwords") are found, while the Catholic dialect has historically drawn many terms from Portuguese. The Catholic literary dialect has now adopted Sanskritic vocabulary itself, and the Catholic Church has also adopted a [Sanskritisation](/wiki/Sanskritisation "Sanskritisation") policy.[[95]](#cite_note-cardona-101) Despite the relative unfamiliarity of the recently introduced Sanskritic vocabulary to the new Catholic generations, there has not been wide resistance to the change.[[95]](#cite_note-cardona-101) On the other hand, southern Konkani dialects, having been influenced by [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada") − one of the languages of Dravidian origin − have undergone re-Sanskritisation over time.[[95]](#cite_note-cardona-101)

## Writing systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Writing systems")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3a/KonkaniNames.PNG)](/wiki/File:KonkaniNames.PNG)

The name *Konkani* in the five scripts it is written in: [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari"), [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_script "Kannada script"), [Latin](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"), [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script").

Main article: [Konkani script](/wiki/Konkani_script "Konkani script")

Konkani has been compelled to become a language using a multiplicity of scripts, and not just one single script used everywhere. This has led to an outward splitting up of the same language, which is spoken and understood by all, despite some inevitable dialectal convergences.[[103]](#cite_note-rais-110)

### Past

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Past")]

Main article: [Goykanadi](/wiki/Goykanadi "Goykanadi")

The [Brahmi script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script") for Konkani fell into disuse.[[104]](#cite_note-111) Later, some inscriptions were written in [old Nagari](/wiki/N%C4%81gar%C4%AB_script "Nāgarī script"). However, owing to the Portuguese conquest in 1510 and the subsequent various restrictions imposed by the Inquisition, some early forms of Devanagari fell out of use in Goa.[[103]](#cite_note-rais-110) The Portuguese promulgated a law banning the use of non-Roman scripts for Konkani in Goa.[[36]](#cite_note-rai-40)

Another script, called *Kandevi* or *Goykandi*, was used for Konkani since the times of the [Kadambas](/wiki/Kadamba_Dynasty "Kadamba Dynasty"), although it lost its popularity after the 17th century. Kandevi/Goykandi is very different from the [Halegannada](/wiki/Halegannada "Halegannada") script, with strikingly similar features.[[105]](#cite_note-archives-112) Unlike Halegannada, Kandevi/Goykandi letters were usually written with a distinctive horizontal bar, like the Nagari scripts. This script may have been evolved out of the [Kadamba script](/wiki/Kadamba_script "Kadamba script"), which was extensively used in Goa and Konkan.[[106]](#cite_note-goy-113) The earliest known inscription in Devanagari dates to 1187 AD.[[76]](#cite_note-Sardesaya-82) The [Roman script](/wiki/Roman_script "Roman script") has the oldest preserved and protected literary tradition, beginning from the 16th century.[[107]](#cite_note-114)

### Present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Present")]

Konkani is written in five scripts: [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari"), [Roman](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"), [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_script "Kannada script"), [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script"), and [Perso-Arabic](/wiki/Perso-Arabic_script "Perso-Arabic script").[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1) Because Devanagari is the official script used to write Konkani in Goa and Maharashtra, most Konkanis (especially Hindus) in those two states write the language in Devanagari. However, Konkani is widely written in the Roman script (called [Romi Konkani](/wiki/Romi_Konkani "Romi Konkani")) by many Konkanis, (especially Catholics).[[65]](#cite_note-konk-69) This is because for many years, all Konkani literature was in the Latin script, and Catholic [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") and other religious literature has always been in the Roman script. Most people of Karnataka use the [Kannada script](/wiki/Kannada_script "Kannada script"); however, the Saraswats of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") use the Devanagari script in the [uttara Kannada](/wiki/North_Kanara "North Kanara") district. [Malayalam script](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script") was used by the Konkani community in Kerala, but there has been a move towards the usage of the Devanagari script in recent years.[[108]](#cite_note-115) [Konkani Muslims](/wiki/Konkani_Muslims "Konkani Muslims") use Arabic script to write Konkani. There has been to trend towards the usage of the Arabic script among Muslim communities; this coincides with them mixing more Urdu and Arabic words into their Konkani dialects.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] When the Sahitya Akademi recognised Konkani in 1975 as an independent and literary language, one of the important factors was the literary heritage of Romi Konkani since the year 1556. However, after Konkani in the Devanagari script was made the official language of Goa in 1987, the Sahitya Akademi has supported only writers in the Devanagari script. For a very long time there has been a rising demand for official recognition of Romi Konkani by Catholics in Goa because a sizeable population of the people in Goa use the Roman script. Also a lot of the content on the Internet and the staging of the famed [Tiatr](/wiki/Tiatr "Tiatr") is written in Romi Konkani. In January 2013, the [Goa Bench](/wiki/Bombay_High_Court#Goa_bench "Bombay High Court") of the Bombay High Court issued a notice to the state government on a [Public Interest Litigation](/wiki/Public_Interest_Litigation "Public Interest Litigation") filed by the [Romi Lipi Action Front](/w/index.php?title=Romi_Lipi_Action_Front&action=edit&redlink=1 "Romi Lipi Action Front (page does not exist)") seeking to amend the Official Language Act to grant official language status to Romi Konkani but has not yet been granted.[[109]](#cite_note-116)

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Konkani_language&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Dialects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Konkani_languages.png/250px-Konkani_languages.png)](/wiki/File:Konkani_languages.png)

Venn diagram of the ISO codes of the Konkani languages

Konkani, despite having a small population, shows a very high number of [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect"). The dialect tree structure of Konkani can easily be classified according to the region, religion, caste, and local tongue influence.[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Based on the historical events and cultural ties of the speakers, [N G Kalelkar](/wiki/Narayan_Govind_Kalelkar "Narayan Govind Kalelkar") has broadly classified the dialects into three main groups:[[1]](#cite_note-MadhaviSardesai-1)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

* **Northern Konkani**: Dialects spoken in [Damaon, Diu & Silvassa](/wiki/Damaon,_Diu_%26_Silvassa "Damaon, Diu & Silvassa") territory and the [Konkan division](/wiki/Konkan_division "Konkan division") of Maharashtra with cultural ties to [Mahratti](/wiki/Mahratti "Mahratti").
* **Central Konkani**: Dialects in Goa, where Konkani came in close contact with [Portuguese history & culture](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India").
* **Southern Konkani**: Dialects spoken in Karnataka, and the [Kassergode](/wiki/Kassergode "Kassergode") district & the [Cochin](/wiki/Cochin "Cochin") district of Kerala. Southern Konkani has absorbed substantial amounts of loanwords from [Kanarese](/wiki/Kanarese "Kanarese"), [Tulu](/wiki/Tulu_language "Tulu language") & [Malabarese](/wiki/Malabarese "Malabarese").
